does: This paper introduces SWE-PRM, a Process Reward Model deployed at inference time to monitor and correct LLM agent trajectories during software engineering task execution. The core contribution is a taxonomy-guided PRM that detects common inefficiencies like redundant exploration, looping, and failure to terminate, then provides interpretable feedback to steer agents back on track without modifying the underlying policy. On SWE-bench Verified, the approach improves task resolution from 40.0% to 50.6% across closed-source models, with particularly strong gains on medium and hard difficulty problems. The key finding is that taxonomy-guided feedback outperforms both unguided and explicit action-prescriptive variants, simultaneously increasing success rates while reducing trajectory length at modest computational cost.
connects: This work builds on recent progress in using reward models for language model guidance, extending beyond the widespread application of outcome reward models (ORMs) that only score final solutions. It responds to growing recognition that LLM agents suffer from execution-time inefficiencies rather than fundamental incapability, drawing on process-level evaluation methods from prior work on verifiable reasoning and trajectory analysis. The paper also connects to the broader literature on intervention and guidance strategies for language model agents, building on work that uses auxiliary models to steer generation, while specifically targeting the software engineering domain where SWE-bench has become a standard evaluation benchmark.
enables: This work opens directions for more sophisticated agent monitoring and course-correction during task execution, potentially informing how future deployed agents can self-correct without external intervention. The taxonomy of common inefficiencies provides a foundation for designing better curriculum learning or pre-training objectives that encourage efficient exploration from the start. More broadly, it demonstrates that interpretable process-level feedback can be more effective than explicit action prescription, suggesting opportunities to develop human-interpretable agent oversight systems and potentially scaling these insights to other complex reasoning domains beyond software engineering where agents must manage long execution horizons.
